coredata.dc:title: Antifouling properties of carbon quantum dots-based electrochemical sensor as a promising platform for highly sensitive detection of insulin
coredata.dc:creator: Abazar, Farbod
Sharifi, Ensiyeh
Noorbakhsh, Abdollah
coredata.dc:description: In this study, for the first time, the performance of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a platform in electrochemical detection of insulin at physiological pH is investigated. Electrochemical oxidation of insulin on the surface of a CQDs-modified glassy carbon (GC) electrode is examined using cyclic voltammetry and hydrodynamic amperometry methods. The results show that the oxidation of insulin at the surface of the normal GC electrode leads to the deactivation of the surface after a short period of time, while a stable and amplified insulin oxidation current is observed using the GC/CQDs modified electrode. The amperometric response of the GC/CQDs electrode provide a low detection limit of 2.24 nM, a sensitivity of 0.87 nA/nM and a linear range of 40–200 nM for insulin determination. Based on suitable stability, antifouling properties and increased insulin oxidation current, CQDs can be used as a new platform for electrochemical detection of insulin at physiological pH. In addition, the facile and low-cost method of preparing CQDs against graphene-based materials such as graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide, can introduce it as a new alternative material in many electrochemical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Insulin detection
Electrochemical sensor
Modified electrode
Antifouling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical oxidation of insulin at GC/CQDs electrode

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was financially supported by University of Isfahan.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) histogram of particle diameter size distribution of as-synthesized CQDs.

Electrochemical impedance plots of the (a) bare GC electrode and (b) GC/CQDs electrode in 0.3 M KCl electrolyte solution containing 10 mM
Fe
CN
6
- 3 / - 4
at an applied potential of 0.22 V and a frequency range of 0.1 to 10 kHz. Symbols are the experimental data and lines show the fitted data based on circuit model shown in the inset.

Cyclic voltammograms of GC electrode in buffer solution (pH 7.0) at scan rate of 10 mV/s (a) in the absence and (b) in the presence 7 μM insulin, (c) and (d) as (a) and (b), respectively for GC/CQDs electrode.

Cyclic voltammograms of GC/CQDs electrode in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) in the presence of 7 μM insulin in different potential scan rates of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 and 100 mV/s (a to j respectively).

Successive cyclic voltammograms of (A) bare GC and (B) GC/CQDs electrodes recorded in 0.1 M PBS (pH7.0) containing 7.0 μM of insulin, at a scan rate of 10 mV/s (interval time between each voltammograms = 1 min).
Cyclic voltammograms GC/CQDs electrode at different concentrations of 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 1.4, 1.8, 2.4, 4, 5, 6, 8 and 10 μM insulin (A to L respectively). Inset shows the relative calibration curve.

(A) Amperometric response of the GC/CQDs electrode for successive additions of different insulin concentration in a stirred solution of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) at an applied potential of 0.72 V. Inset shows the resulting calibration plot. (B) As (A) for five time successive additions of 40 nM insulin. Inset shows the plot of amperometric response versus insulin concentration.
Stability of amperometric response of (a) GC/CQDs and (b) bare GC electrodes in a stirred solution of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 7 μM insulin during 600 s at an applied potential of 0.72 V.

Cyclic voltammograms of the GC/CQDs electrode in 0.1 PBS (pH 7.0) in the presence of 2 and 3 µM insulin (a and b, respectively), after adding the 1 ml of human blood serum to the electrolyte containing 3 µM insulin (c) and in the presence of 4 and 5 µM insulin with human blood serum at scan rate of 10 mV/s (d and e, respectively). Inset shows the plot of anodic current versus insulin concentration.
Comparison for the performance characteristic of insulin sensor in this work with other modified electrodes reported.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ITO indium tin oxide, AgNFs silver nano flowers, SCE screen printed electrode, GCE glassy carbon electrode, MWCNT multi-wall carbon nano tube, rGO reduced grapheme oxide, CNT carbon nano tube, CCE carbon ceramic electrode, SPE screen printed electrode, CPE carbon paste electrode, GQD grapheme quantum dot, GN graphite nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Antifouling properties of carbon quantum dots-based electrochemical sensor as a promising platform for highly sensitive detection of insulin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanotechnology, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Isfahan, Isfahan 81746-73441, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanotechnology, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Isfahan, Isfahan 81746-73441, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, for the first time, the performance of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a platform in electrochemical detection of insulin at physiological pH is investigated. Electrochemical oxidation of insulin on the surface of a CQDs-modified glassy carbon (GC) electrode is examined using cyclic voltammetry and hydrodynamic amperometry methods. The results show that the oxidation of insulin at the surface of the normal GC electrode leads to the deactivation of the surface after a short period of time, while a stable and amplified insulin oxidation current is observed using the GC/CQDs modified electrode. The amperometric response of the GC/CQDs electrode provide a low detection limit of 2.24 nM, a sensitivity of 0.87 nA/nM and a linear range of 40–200 nM for insulin determination. Based on suitable stability, antifouling properties and increased insulin oxidation current, CQDs can be used as a new platform for electrochemical detection of insulin at physiological pH. In addition, the facile and low-cost method of preparing CQDs against graphene-based materials such as graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide, can introduce it as a new alternative material in many electrochemical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are quasi-spherical nanostructured carbon particles with diameters below 10 nm [1,2]. CQDs have attracted many researchers’ attention due to their potential in various applications since their accidental discovery by Xu et al [3]. In 2004 during single-walled carbon nanotubes purification. CQDs possess strong application in photocatalysis [4], bioimaging [5], biosensing [6], etc; due to their photoluminescence properties, high stability, low toxicity, low cost and simple synthetic routes [7]. In addition, owing to their large surface area and interesting catalytic properties, CQDs can be used as an effective material for electrode modification in electrochemical sensing [8]. The synthesis and application of these new interesting materials in biosensing have been reviewed in recent literatures [9,10]. Electrochemical sensing strategies provide many advantages, including simplicity, high sensitivity, fast analysis, low detection limit, ease of use and low-cost analysis. However, there are only a few reports for the application of CQDs in electroanalysis [10]. Cu–N-doped CQDs [11], CQD-PGEs [8], poly(β-cyclodextrin)/CQDs [2], poly (bromocresol green)/CQDs [6] were used for electrochemical determination of glucose, l-cysteine, uric acid, daunorubicin, guanine and adenine. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanoparticles modified glassy carbon electrode accelerated electron transfer and exhibited excellent synergistic electrochemical activity for determination of quercetin in biological samples [12]. A modified glassy carbon with CQDs-chitosan film has been reported to increase electrochemical performance toward dopamine determination [13]. Moreover, electrochemistry of GQDs and CQDs using basal-plane pyrolytic graphite electrodes have been compared, while CQDs displayed superior electrocatalytic properties in comparison with GQDs [14]. One of the key factors for the fabrication of low-cost sensors/biosensors is employing low-cost and available materials [10,15,16]. CQDs can be synthesized easily from very low-cost and available materials such as candle and natural resources [9,12]. In the present work, a facile, low-cost, and simple strategy was used to synthesize CQDs, as very suitable materials, for electrocatalytic determination of insulin biomarker as a model analyte.

Insulin is a polypeptide hormone produced by pancreatic b-cells, which regulates carbohydrate and fat metabolism in the body. The insufficient secretion of insulin or loss of insulin activity at target tissues leads to severe metabolism disorder named diabetes, and patients suffering from this type of illness face increased risk factors for serious health problems, including kidney failure, myocardial infarction, obesity and neurodegenerative disease [17]. Therefore, frequent monitoring of insulin level for diabetes patients to avoid insulin deficiency is necessary. In addition, the detection of insulin is of great importance for clinical diagnostics as a predictor for diabetes, insulinoma and trauma [18,19]. Due to diabetes prevalence, it is likely to lead to a 61% increase in worldwide numbers by 2030 [20]; developing a simple and cost-efficient method for detecting insulin in blood serum is a topic of great interest in developing countries.

So far, many different methods for insulin determination have already been reported, such as immunoassay [21], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [22], capillary electrophoresis [23] and chromatography techniques [24]. Even though these methods are effective, but they are slow, time-consuming, high cost and required relevant equipment [25]. Electrochemical detection of insulin has several advantages compared to other methods, such as high sensitivity, low cost, rapid response and, more importantly, the capability of design and construction of portable devices easy to use for point of care [26,27].

Unfortunately, surface fouling of conventional electrodes during insulin oxidation known as a major limitation for electrochemical detection of insulin [28,29]. This limitation is due to the adsorption of the thiol radicals as products of the sulfur amino acid’s electrochemical reaction process in insulin compound, which results in rapid deterioration and decreases the stability of the electrode [30]. As a result, to overcome this limitation and achieving a suitable electrochemical insulin sensor, various chemically modified electrodes have been studied. Several redox mediators and electrocatalysts have been reported as surface modifiers to overcome surface fouling limitation, such as ruthenium oxide/cyanoruthenate (RuO/CN-Ru) [31], ruthenium-oxide-type catalytic film (RuOx) [32], and iridium oxide (IrOx) [33]. Although these electrodes have been widely and successfully used to measure insulin levels. But the main problem with these sensors is the reduced stability in physiological conditions and gradual surface contamination due to insulin oxidation [34]. Zhang, et al. Have investigated a different mechanism of insulin oxidation at the CNT-coated electrode surface via the reaction of tyrosine components, which can be more stable than previous electrodes [35]. In our previous works, reduced graphene oxide modified electrode exhibited impressive high stability and antifouling properties toward insulin oxidation [36]. In this work, the CQDs as novel materials have been investigated for the first time in insulin electrochemical sensor. A very simple, cost effective and facile route for the synthesis of CQDs was proposed for modification of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) in order to insulin detection. To the best of our knowledge, there have been no reports on the development of electrochemical determination of insulin by using CQDs modified electrode. Antifouling, stability and signal amplification characteristics of CQDs toward oxidation of insulin were investigated using cyclic voltammetry and amperometric techniques. Experimental conditions for the amperometric detection of insulin were optimized and analytical performances of the modified electrode were evaluated. The obtained results demonstrate the potential of synthesized CQDs as a low-cost and high-performance material for electrochemical sensing of insulin.

Recombinant human insulin (rHu) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA, 5,807.65 g/mol). Candles were obtained from the local market. Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), potassium chloride (KCl), potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6), potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Merck. The stock solution of insulin (0.5 mM) was daily prepared from its powder using phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7). Insulin solutions were supplied by diluting the insulin stock solution to a convenient concentration in the same buffer. Human serum samples were obtained from healthy volunteers. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as purchased without further purification. All solutions were prepared with double distilled water.

For characterization of the as-synthesized CQDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were carried out using a Philips CM200 operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of solid-state samples (KBr matrix) were recorded between 400 and 4000 cm−1 on a Jasco 6300 FTIR spectrometer. Cyclic voltammetry and amperometric measurements were carried out using a computer-controlled μ-Autolab modular electrochemical system (Eco Chemie Ultecht, The Netherlands), run with NOVA software. A three-electrode system with an Ag/AgCl (saturated) 3 M KCl as a reference electrode, a platinum wire as a counter electrode and a glassy carbon (GC) electrode (2 mm in diameter) modified with CQDs (prepared as follows) as a working electrode in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7) was used for all electrochemical experiments. A Metrohm drive shaft was used in amperometric detections to rotate working electrodes. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments were carried out in a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 10 mM Fe(CN)6 -3/-4 (1:1) with frequency range from 10 kHz to 0.1 Hz, at a potential of 0.22 V, with an oscillation potential of 5 mV. All of the electrochemical experiments were performed at pH 7 and room temperature (25.0 + 0.1 °C).

In recent years, much literature reports the using of candle soot in preparating of carbon nanoparticles [37–39]. Energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDX) analysis reveal that carbon soot particles commonly contain carbon and oxygen elements with more that 85% w/w carbon [39]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized in the present work were prepared from candle soot using a very simple and one step solvent route [12]. Briefly, carbon black was obtained by burning a candle. Then, 4 mg of as-prepared carbon black was dissolved in 7 ml N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and sonicated for 3 h. At last, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was collected as the CQDs and dried at 100 °C for 2 h. The CQDs suspention was prepared by dissolving a certain amount of CQDs powder in distilled water and sonicated for 3 h.

Before each modification, the GC electrode was polished on a polishing surface with 0.05 µM alumina slurry, rinsed with distilled water, and sonicated in 99% ethanol for several minutes to remove any residues adsorbed on the surface. The synthesized CQDs powder was dispersed in 1 ml distilled water with a concentration of 3 mg/ml followed by 3 h sonication to yield a homogenous black suspension. The GC/CQDs electrode was prepared by drop casting 2 µl of the CQDs suspension on the GC electrode surface and letting it dry in air. This step was repeated four times to ensure the formation of an effective layer on the surface. The obtained GC/CQDs electrode was rinsed with distilled water to remove unbound particles from the surface.

TEM image taken from the as-prepared CQDs shows the formation of a monodispersed spherical nanoparticles with uniform shapes (Fig. 1 a). The histogram of particle size distribution (Fig. 1b) proposes a relatively narrow size range of CQDs with average diameters less than 10 nm. From these results, the proposed synthesis method can be used as a good method for successful synthesis of CQDs with relatively narrow size and uniform shape.

FTIR spectra analysis was also performed to investigate the chemical structure and functional groups of CQDs (Fig. 2 ). The presence of a broad and intense peak around 3326 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of O–H, indicating the existence of large numbers of hydroxyl groups on the surface [40]. The sharp peak at 1032 cm−1 is attributed to the C-O-C stretching vibration [41]. The double peaks at 1720 cm−1 and 1470 cm−1 are corresponded to the CO and O–H, respectively [42]. The existence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface resulted in excellent solubility and stability of CQDs nanoparticles in aqueous solutions without further chemical modifications. Also, the absorption peak at around 1617 cm−1 attributed to the CC stretching of carbon indicates the sp2 hybridized honeycomb skeleton of the CQDs [41,43].

EIS experiments were performed to characterize the charge transfer properties of CQDs modified electrodes. Nyquist impedance plots consist of two main parts, a semicircle part in high frequencies, related to charge-transfer resistance, and a linear portion in low frequencies, related to mass diffusion limited process. The charge-transfer resistance (R ct) on Nyquist impedance plots is equal to the diameter of the semicircle portion. R ct values were also estimated by fitting the plot using the standard Randles equivalent circuit. As shown in Fig. 3 , the R ct of the GC electrode surface decreased significantly from 255.74 Ω to 14.05 Ω after modification with CQDs. This result indicated excellent conductivity of CQDs that resulted in superior electron transfer kinetics at GC/CQDs surface. Therefore, CQDs could be an appropriate choice for the fabrication of modified electrode as an electron conducting network to facilitate the electron transfer process at the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical oxidation of insulin at GC/CQDs electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of insulin oxidation at the surface of as-prepared GC/CQDs electrode was investigated using the cyclic voltammetry method. Fig. 4 shows the cyclic voltammograms of GC and GC/CQDs electrodes in the absence and presence of 7 µM insulin in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7). After modification of the electrode with CQD, the background current obviously increased as a result of the incensement of the surface area and double layer capacitance at the modified electrode [44,45]. In the presence of insulin, an anodic peak related to the direct oxidation of insulin was observed in both electrodes. However, the modified GC/CQDs electrode showed a significant lower overpotential for the oxidation of insulin compared to the bare GC electrode. Electrochemical oxidation current of insulin started from 0.57 V and reached to the maximum value at 0.67 V, while for GC/CQDs these values were found to be 0.44 V and 0.55 V, respectively. Also, for the modified GC/CQDs electrode a very larger anodic peak current (about 4-fold) can be observed compared to the unmodified GC electrode. These results indicated high catalytic activity of CQDs for the electrochemical oxidation of insulin, leading to a dramatic enhancement of insulin oxidation current and substantial decrease of oxidation overpotential. No redox reaction was observed for both GC and GC/CQD electrodes in the absence of insulin.

Velasco equation can be used for direct determination of the standard rate constant (k) of irreversible electrochemical reactions on the electrode surface from cyclic/linear sweep voltammograms. Equation (1) is used for determination of k for irreversible electrochemical reaction at any temperature by measuring Ep - Ep/2, [46].

(1)
k = 2.415
exp
- 0.02
F
RT
D o
1 2
E p
-
E
p / 2
- 1 / 2
ν
1 / 2
where Ep/2 is the half peak potential, Ep is the peak potential, υ is the potential scan rate and Do is the diffusion coefficient of electroactive species, F is the Faraday constant, R is the gas constant, T is the temperature in K, and v is the potential sweep rate. For the case of room temperature (T = 298 K), the equation (2) is obtained and used for determination of k:
(2)
k = 1.11
D o
1 2
E p
-
E
p / 2
- 1 / 2
ν
1 / 2

Many literatures used the velasco equation for determination of heterogeneous standard rate constant of irreversible electrochemical reactions [47–49]. In the present work, the apparent charge-transfer rate constant of the GC/CQDs electrode for the oxidation of insulin was evaluated via cyclic voltammetry. For this purpose, the anodic peak current of the GC/CQDs electrode was recorded at the scan rate of 20 mV/s in the presence of 7 µM insulin in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7). The k value can be obtained using Velasco equation (Eq. (2)). The calculated k value at GC/CQDs electrode was found to be 6.67 × 10-4 cm/s, which is comparable to the value reported in literature for the electrocatalytic oxidation of insulin at the reduced graphene oxide-modified GC (GC/rGO) electrode (7.08 × 10-4 cm/s) [36]. These results indicate the excellent capability of CQDs for efficient electrochemical oxidation of insulin [36]. For more investigation of electron transfer process, cyclic voltammograms of GC/CQDs electrode were recorded at different scan rates (10 to 100 mV/s) in the presence of 5 µM insulin in 0.1 M PBS (Fig. 5 ). A plot of peak currents versus square roots of scan rate is presented in the inset of Fig. 5. The Linear range obtained from the plot according to Eq. (3) [50], where Ip is the peak current (A), A is the effective surface area of the electrode (cm2), Do is the diffusion coefficient (cm2/s), n is the number of total electrons, α is the electron transfer coefficient, nα is the number of electrons in the rate-determining step and Co is the concentration of the electroactive species (mol/cm3), suggested a diffusion controlled electrochemical behavior. Also, the insignificant change of insulin oxidation potential with increasing scan rate showed fast electron transfer kinetics of the process at the GC/CQD electrode surface.
(3)
I p
=
2.99 ×
10 5
n A
C o
D o
1 2
α
n α
1 / 2
ν
1 / 2

By recording the cyclic voltammograms of GC and GC/CQDs electrodes in a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 10 mM Fe(CN)6 -3/-4 (1:1) as an electrochemical probe, and by using the Randles–Sevcik equation, the active surface area of the GC and GC/CQDs electrodes were calculated to be 0.041 and 0.053 cm2, respectively, which is larger than that of GC and lower than the value reported for GC/rGO in our previous work (the diffusion coefficient of Fe(CN)6 -3/-4 is assumed to be 7.3 ± 0.7 × 10─6 cm2/s) [36]. In other experiments performed in this study, the active surface area of GC and GC/CQDs electrodes were calculated from the Eq. (3) by using insulin as the electrochemical probe. The active surface area of GC and GC/CQDs electrodes were calculated to be 0.030 and 0.048 cm2, respectively. It should be noted that by modification of GC electrode by CQDs, the active surface area of the electrode increased about 1.5 fold, while the electrochemical oxidation current of insulin increased about 4 fold (Fig. 4), representing efficient electrocatalytic activity of CQDs toward the oxidation of insulin.

Antifouling properties of CQDs as an electrode surface modifier for insulin oxidation were investigated and compared with bare GC electrode. Fig. 6 shows successive cyclic voltammograms of bare GC electrode and GC/CQDs in the presence of 7 µM insulin in 0.1 M PBS (7 pH). The anodic peak of insulin oxidation at bare GC electrode surface tended to disappear after several cycles, which can be related to the adhesion of insulin oxidation products on the electrode surface. Under similar conditions unlike bare GC electrode, cyclic voltammograms of GC/CQDs electrode remained unchanged after 9 cycles and a very stable response was observed for the electrooxidation of insulin at the surface of this modified electrode. These results clearly demonstrated the antifouling property of CQDs toward the oxidation of insulin. Antifouling properties of CQDs are comparable to the results previously reported based on carbon nanomaterials such as rGO [36]. In addition, simple, fast and low-cost synthesis of CQDs, fast electron transfer kinetics of insulin oxidation GC/CQDs, and simple GC/CQDs electrode preparation make CQDs on alternative suitable material for the fabrication of insulin electrochemical sensors.

Cyclic voltammetry and amperometric measurements were carried out to examine the performance of the developed sensor for insulin determination. Cyclic voltammograms of the GC/CQDs electrode in various insulin concentrations (from 0.4 µM to 10 µM) were recorded and investigated. Fig. 7 shows a considerable change in the anodic peak current upon the increasing of insulin concentration. The inset of Fig. 7 shows the corresponding calibration curve of the proposed insulin sensor. The oxidation peak of insulin increased linearly with increasing insulin concentration in the range of 0 to 2.4 µM. Considering the linear part of the graph (equation (4)), detection limit (S/N = 3) and sensitivity of the sensor were estimated to be 22.88 nM and 1.3691 µA/µM, respectively.
(4)
I
μ A
= 1.3691
Insulin
μ M
- 0.2725
μ A
R 2
:
0.9995

In order to achieve higher sensitivity and lower detection limit, the amperometric technique with rotating modified GC/CQDs electrode at a constant potential of 0.72 V and rotating speed of 1000 r.p.m was employed for electrochemical determination of various insulin concentrations in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7). As shown in Fig. 8 , after successive addition of 40 nM insulin, a fast, well-defined, and steady-state current response was observed. From the plot of current response versus insulin concentration (the inset of Fig. 8), the sensor response increased linearly by increasing insulin concentration from 40 to 200 nM (Eq. (5)).
(5)
I
μ A
= 0.8768
INS
nM
+ 5.99
nA
R 2
:
0.9997

Based on equation (5), the detection limit (S/N = 3) and sensitivity of the proposed sensor were found to be 2.24 nM and 0.8768 nA/nM, respectively. The detection limit, linear range, and sensitivity of the proposed sensor are comparable to various electrochemical insulin sensors (Table 1 ). These results are more impressive considering significant advantages of the developed sensor including very simple and fast sensor preparation method as well as highly available and extremely low cost of initial materials used for synthesis of CQDs as electrode substrate materials.

The response stability of the fabricated insulin sensors was investigated by using amperometry technique. As shown in Fig. 9 , the amperometric response of GC/CQDs recorded in stirred solution containing 7 µM insulin decreased less than 4% after 14 min. While under the same conditions, the amperometric response of the bare GC electrode decreased about 50 % after only 20 s and no recognizable response was observed after 100 s. These results revealed the great antifouling properties of CQDs for the oxidation of insulin. Also, the proposed sensor showed acceptable long-term stability and maintained about 98% of its initial response to the insulin after 5 days (Fig. S2), implying the satisfactory storage stability of the developed sensor. The developed sensor showed a standard deviation (RSD) of 6.3% for electrochemical oxidation of insulin at the surface of 5 individual electrodes (Fig. S3).

To investigate the practical efficiency of the developed sensor, the electrochemical detection of insulin in the absence and presence of human serum samples was studied using cyclic voltammetry. As shown in Fig. 10 , cyclic voltammograms of GC/CQDs electrode were recorded in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7) with successive addition of insulin in each cycle. The first two cycles (voltammograms a and b) were recorded in the presence of 2 and 3 µM insulin, respectively, followed by addition of 1 ml blood serum sample (voltammogram c), and 4 and 5 µM insulin (voltammograms d and e, respectively). After the addition of 1 ml blood serum to the electrolyte solution containing 3 µM insulin, the insulin oxidation current remained unchanged, while a new anodic wave was observed at 0.33 V related to oxidation of ascorbic acid and uric acid known as interference biological species (IBS). Due to the wide difference between insulin and IBS oxidation potential, this electrode demonstrated non-interference of insulin oxidation peak with IBS, representing CQDs as suitable nanostructures for use in detection of insulin in human blood serum. The correlation plot of anodic current versus insulin concentration is shown in the inset of Fig. 10. The linear response (the correlation coefficient is 0.9915) obtained for a combination of two standard samples and two samples in the presence of human blood serum indicate appropriate properties of the proposed sensor for the determination of insulin in practical samples.

In another experiments, various concentration of insulin were spiked in the human blood samples for testing the recovery. The recovery values, shown in Table 2 , were found to be ranged between 93% and 104%, indicating the reliability of the proposed sensor for the determination of insulin in real samples.

In this work, the electrochemical oxidation of insulin at the surface of the glassy carbon modified with CQDs obtained from a facile synthesis method was investigated for the first time. CQDs showed a very good antifouling properties for sensitive electrochemical determination of insulin. Also, due to the high surface area and catalytic activity of CQDs, GC/CQDs electrode exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity and excellent stability toward the oxidation of insulin. Modification of introduced electrode is much more straightforward, fast, and inexpensive compared to the recently reported carbon-nanomaterial based-modified electrodes. Due to the similarity of the CQDs and rGO electrochemical performance, CQDs can be used as a suitable alternative for carbon based nanomaterials. Besides the excellent properties described above, GC/CQDs can be used to detect insulin with a low detection limit of 2.24 nM and a linear range from 40 nM to 200 nM in physiological pH. Outstanding antifouling properties of GC/CQDs toward the oxidation of insulin as well as its excellent selectivity in human serum sample solutions were found as other great features of the developed insulin sensor. These results demonstrated that CQDs can be used as a low-cost, high-performance material alternative for graphene-based products in electrode surface modifications for electrochemical sensing applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was financially supported by University of Isfahan.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107560.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications

Dong, Poly (β-cyclodextrin)/carbon quantum dots modified glassy carbon electrode: preparation, characterization and simultaneous electrochemical determination of dopamine, uric acid and tryptophan, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 252 (2017) 9-16.
